← the Atlas

Graph view + Maps of Content

feature · budding ·implemented ·JUN 19, 2026

The Atlas index IS a force-directed graph — the four kinds become hub nodes, every note edges to its kind, and the Maps of Content fall straight out of the existing links. One entry point, everything reachable.

Contents

  • Kinds are hubs, not a separate skeleton
  • It reuses the links the Atlas already has
  • How the page stays a self-contained file
  • Where this could go

The Atlas index is the graph. Instead of a categorical tree plus a separate graph page, there is one entry point: a force-directed graph of every note, with a Maps of Content strip below it. The graph answers “what’s connected to what,” the hubs answer “where do I start,” and together they reach every note — there is no second index to keep in sync.

Open the Atlas. Hover a node to trace its neighborhood, click to open the note, drag/scroll to move around.

Kinds are hubs, not a separate skeleton

The old index had a hardcoded skeleton — one section per kind. The graph dissolves that: each of the four kinds (bug · feature · analysis · reference) is a synthetic hub node, and every note carries an implicit edge to its kind. Two things fall out for free:

  • Nothing is unfiled and nothing is an orphan. Every note is connected — to its kind hub at minimum — so the graph is one piece and every node is reachable by traversal, not just the well-linked ones.
  • The Maps of Content are complete. A kind hub’s cluster is exactly the notes of that kind, so the four kind cards together name every note. That’s what lets the page be browsed — and every node reached — with no JavaScript at all.

High-degree topical notes (electricity, solid-fileview, pty-daemon, …) surface as hubs the same way — by degree — so a cross-cutting entry point is discovered, never declared.

It reuses the links the Atlas already has

No new frontmatter. The real edges are exactly the two the Atlas already authors — parents and same-directory ./slug.html prose links — derived by the very same pass that powers the dead-link gate and the “Referenced by” backlinks. The kind edges are synthetic, added only in the graph layout. A node’s degree sets its size; its inbound count ranks it as a topical hub.

How the page stays a self-contained file

Each Atlas page is a committed, self-contained HTML file that previews in kolu’s Code tab with no dev server, and the build is a pure function of source (the ci::atlas-sync gate rebuilds under a scrambled timezone/locale and fails on any drift). The graph honors both:

  • Layout is computed at build time with d3-force and baked into a static inline <svg> as rounded coordinates. Nothing lays out at runtime.
  • Determinism is engineered in: nodes are sorted by id and edges by pair-key before the simulation, positions are seeded by that stable index, a fixed number of ticks runs synchronously, and every coordinate is rounded. d3-force v3 carries no Math.random, so the baked geometry is byte-identical on every rebuild.
  • The only client JS is one is:inline script for interaction (zoom/pan, hover-highlight, click-to-open) — so no _astro bundle is emitted and the page stays one file. Nodes are real <a> links and the Maps-of-Content cards are plain linked lists, so the page reads and navigates with JavaScript off.

Why one view instead of two

A tree and a graph over the same notes are two things to keep in sync, and the tree only ever showed the skeleton the graph now expresses as structure. Folding them into one entry point means a single data source — the link graph — drives the picture and the browsable index, so they can never disagree, and “navigate to any note” is guaranteed by construction.

Where this could go

  • ✓ unifyGraph + Maps of Content as the single index; kinds as synthetic hub nodes so every note is connected and reachable; build-time d3-force baked to a self-contained SVG.
  • ○ 1Filter/focus by kind or maturity; isolate a single hub’s cluster in place.
  • ○ 2Flag weakly-linked notes (only the kind edge) as a “wire these up” nudge — now that true orphans are gone, near-orphans are the signal.
  • ○ 3Deep-link into the graph centered on the note you came from (a “see this in context” link from each note).

The index, rethought — one graph over the existing link structure. The link graph is the source of truth; this is the way to see and walk it.
